editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Viet Nam, Venezuela, RB, Uzbekistan, while the lightest include South Sudan, Central African Republic, Chad; that contrast is the fastest way to read the urban electricity access pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Urban electricity access can reveal where cities are still not fully connected to basic power.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Electricity access can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Rural electricity access can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Urban basic sanitation can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Standard difficulty: the map has readable regional structure, but the best answer still depends on checking the unit and country anchors.
regionalSignals: Settlement maps reward checking dense city-states and large rural countries.
Urban and rural shares are mirror-image clues.

reviewNotes: Batch 3 QA: clear source-valid urban access map, but close to national electricity access.
Practice-only until urban/rural access variants are tested.

editorial.shortHook: A candidate map for urban population growth.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Burundi, Chad, Rwanda, while the lightest include Moldova, Latvia, Kuwait; that contrast is the fastest way to read the urban population growth pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Urban population growth may add useful WORLDPRINT variety once source coverage, unit clarity, map interest, and distractor ambiguity are reviewed.
